evidence: No implementation exists anywhere in the codebase; mcp_server.py dispatches to canned-data managers with no state-machine logic
diagnosis: The remediation ladder (rollout restart -> wait 5 min -> escalate) is completely unimplemented; no state machine, no timer, no escalation path exists.
proposed_fix: Model a 3-state machine: PENDING -> RESTARTING -> VERIFYING -> ESCALATED. Persist state in a dict keyed by (namespace, deployment). On entry to RESTARTING call `kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>`; start asyncio.create_task with a 300-second sleep; on wakeup call `kubectl rollout status` — if success transition to VERIFIED, else transition to ESCALATED and emit an alert event. Edge cases: deployment not found, rollout already in progress, multiple concurrent ladders for the same target.
test_plan: Unit-test each transition with a mock kubectl runner returning canned rollout-status output; integration-test against a real cluster by injecting a crashlooping deployment and asserting ESCALATED state fires within 310 seconds.

evidence: No such loop exists in the codebase; src/mcp_server.py is a single-pass tool-dispatch server with no iteration concept
diagnosis: The review->PRD->split->implement->verify loop with stop conditions is entirely absent from the current design; there is no persistent iteration state, no stop condition evaluation, and no feedback between verify and the next review.
proposed_fix: Define an IterationRecord dataclass: {id, phase: Literal[REVIEW,PRD,SPLIT,IMPLEMENT,VERIFY], stop_reason: Optional[str], created_at, updated_at}. Persist in SQLite or a JSON file. Transitions are driven by MCP tool calls (e.g. `start_iteration`, `advance_iteration`, `stop_iteration`). Stop conditions: MAX_ITERATIONS=10, all verify checks green, or explicit operator abort. Each VERIFY phase runs pytest and checks for empty failed-pods list; failure rewinds to IMPLEMENT.
test_plan: Write a pytest fixture that drives the state machine through one full cycle using mock tool responses; assert stop condition fires at MAX_ITERATIONS; assert rewind from VERIFY->IMPLEMENT when tests fail.

evidence: deploy-k8s.sh:6 — `set -e` only; missing `-u` (unbound variables silently expand to empty) and `-o pipefail` (pipeline failures silently succeed); line 63: `CLUSTER_SERVER=$(kubectl cluster-info | grep ... | awk ...)` — pipeline failure in grep/awk is swallowed
diagnosis: Script uses only `set -e`, leaving unbound-variable expansion and pipeline errors uncaught, which can silently produce wrong values for CLUSTER_SERVER and NODEPORT and cause incorrect deployment behavior.
proposed_fix: Change line 6 from `set -e` to `set -euo pipefail`. Quote all variable expansions (lines 69, 73, 75, 133, 136, 137). Replace the bare `read -p` prompt (line 106) with an explicit check of stdin being a tty. Validate that NODEPORT is non-empty before using it in output at line 164.
test_plan: Run `bash -n deploy-k8s.sh` to catch syntax errors; then run with `bash -x` against a kind cluster and confirm no empty-variable substitutions appear in trace output.

evidence: k8s/ingress.yaml:11 — `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "false"`; lines 15-19: TLS block commented out; no securityContext, no resource limits, no RBAC annotations, no rate-limiting annotations
diagnosis: Ingress is intentionally configured with TLS disabled and SSL redirect off, exposing the MCP server (which may handle kubeconfig credentials) over plain HTTP with no rate limiting or auth annotations.
proposed_fix: Set `ssl-redirect: "true"`. Uncomment the TLS block and wire a cert-manager ClusterIssuer. Add `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps: "10"` and `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-type: basic` (or equivalent OAuth2-proxy annotation). Add a `kubernetes.io/ingress.class` label for explicit controller binding. Document the cert-manager prerequisite in a comment.
test_plan: Apply the manifest to a test cluster with cert-manager installed; assert the Certificate resource reaches Ready state; curl the HTTP endpoint and assert 301 redirect to HTTPS; curl HTTPS endpoint and assert 200.

evidence: src/mcp_server.py:577-579 dispatches to `self.documentation_manager.get_troubleshooting_guide`; documentation_manager.py:401-413 iterates `self.documentation_db` which is populated only by live HTTP scraping of kubernetes.io at startup (documentation_manager.py:51-66); if startup HTTP calls fail or return no content, `documentation_db` is empty and the method returns `[]`
diagnosis: get_troubleshooting_guide returns an empty list whenever the live kubernetes.io scrape fails at startup; there is no fallback, no offline cache, and no error surfaced to the caller — the tool silently returns no results.
proposed_fix: Bundle a minimal static troubleshooting index as a JSON file in the repo (data/troubleshooting_index.json). In `_build_documentation_index`, seed `documentation_db` from the static file before attempting live scrape. Wrap the live scrape in a try/except that logs a warning but does not raise. In `get_troubleshooting_guide`, return a structured error dict (not empty list) when results are empty so the MCP caller can surface it.
test_plan: Unit-test with aiohttp mocked to raise ConnectionError; assert the method returns at least the static-index entries. Integration-test with real kubernetes.io reachable; assert at least one guide is returned for issue_type='pod'.

evidence: security_manager.py:93-116 (_scan_rbac), 118-150 (_scan_pod_security), 152-176 (_scan_network_policies), 178-201 (_scan_secrets), 203-232 (_scan_container_images) — every private scan method returns hardcoded dict literals with fabricated resource names ('debug-pod', 'nginx:1.18', 'db-credentials') and fixed counts; no kubernetes client import anywhere in the file
diagnosis: All five security scan sub-methods return entirely fabricated data; the security_manager never imports or calls the kubernetes client, making every security result a lie regardless of the real cluster state.
proposed_fix: Import `kubernetes.client` and `kubernetes.config`. In `initialize()`, call `kubernetes.config.load_incluster_config()` with fallback to `load_kube_config()`. Replace `_scan_rbac` to list ClusterRoleBindings via `RbacAuthorizationV1Api` and check for wildcard verbs. Replace `_scan_pod_security` to iterate pods via `CoreV1Api.list_pod_for_all_namespaces` and inspect securityContext. Replace `_scan_secrets` to list secrets and check for unencrypted data values. Replace `_scan_container_images` with a call to an external trivy or grype binary.
test_plan: Unit-test each scan method with kubernetes client mocked to return a known resource set; assert findings match expected output. Integration-test against a real cluster with a known-misconfigured pod and assert it appears in results.

evidence: No done_definition check exists in any source file; security_manager, diagnostics_manager, and monitoring_manager all return hardcoded data so 'proven with output' cannot be verified against real cluster state
diagnosis: There is no codified definition of DONE that can be automatically evaluated; the required conditions (green pytest, image build success, clean events, empty failed pods) are not checked anywhere programmatically.
proposed_fix: Implement a `DoneChecker` class with four methods: `check_pytest()` runs `pytest --tb=short -q` and asserts returncode==0; `check_image_build()` runs `docker build --no-cache` and asserts returncode==0; `check_events()` calls CoreV1Api.list_event_for_all_namespaces filtering type=Warning and asserts count==0 for the target namespace; `check_failed_pods()` calls CoreV1Api.list_pod_for_all_namespaces filtering phase=Failed and asserts count==0. A `is_done()` method returns True only when all four pass and logs which check failed.
test_plan: Unit-test each check method with mocked subprocess and kubernetes client; assert is_done() returns False when any single check fails; integration-test by deliberately failing a pod and asserting check_failed_pods() returns False.

evidence: kubectl_manager.py:83-91 — `_mask_secrets` regex on line 87 uses a pattern that matches only a single base64 value per `data:` block and requires specific whitespace (`(?:\s+\w+:\s+)`), so multi-key secret blocks are not fully masked; line 254 — `kubectl_generic` splits command by whitespace (`command.split()`) without shell-quote awareness, allowing arguments with spaces to be silently mangled
diagnosis: The secret masking regex is brittle and incomplete, risking credential leakage in multi-key secrets, and the generic kubectl command splitter breaks on arguments containing spaces.
proposed_fix: Replace `_mask_secrets` with a YAML-aware parser: load the output with `yaml.safe_load` and recursively replace all values under 'data' and 'stringData' keys with '<MASKED>' before re-serializing. Replace `command.split()` in `kubectl_generic` with `shlex.split(command)` to handle quoted arguments correctly.
test_plan: Unit-test `_mask_secrets` with a multi-key secret YAML string and assert all values are masked; unit-test `kubectl_generic` with a command containing a label selector using spaces (e.g. `get pods -l 'app=foo,env=bar'`) and assert the subprocess receives the correct arg list.

evidence: coming_soon.md:195 — 'API response time under 2 seconds for 99% of requests'; coming_soon.md:179 — 'Test coverage maintained above 95%'; coming_soon.md:79 — 'Zero-trust architecture implementation guidance'; coming_soon.md:80 — 'Compliance automation for SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA' — none of these are implemented; the document frames aspirational goals as current-state metrics under the heading 'Metrics & Success Criteria'
diagnosis: coming_soon.md presents future aspirations as current success criteria without any caveats, creating false impressions about the server's current performance, compliance posture, and test coverage.
proposed_fix: Rename the 'Metrics & Success Criteria' section to 'Target Metrics (Not Yet Measured)'. Add a one-line disclaimer at the top: 'All items in this document are planned features; none are implemented in the current release.' Prefix each metric bullet with 'Target:' to signal it is aspirational. Remove or caveat the compliance automation bullets (SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA) until a compliance framework is actually wired.
test_plan: Review the updated document and confirm no present-tense claims remain for unimplemented features; verify that a new contributor reading it cannot mistake roadmap items for shipped functionality.

evidence: No DPO pair extraction exists anywhere in the codebase; no rejected_actions or chosen_action data model is defined in any source file
diagnosis: DPO pair extraction (rejected_actions, chosen_action, evidence) -> GitHub issue body is completely undesigned and unimplemented.
proposed_fix: Define a dataclass: `DPOPair(rejected_actions: List[str], chosen_action: str, evidence: str, context: Dict[str, Any])`. Implement `emit_issue_body(pair: DPOPair) -> str` that produces a markdown GitHub issue body with sections: '## Context' (pair.context as a code block), '## Chosen Action' (pair.chosen_action), '## Rejected Alternatives' (bulleted list of pair.rejected_actions), '## Evidence' (pair.evidence). A `DPOCollector` class wraps tool-call execution, records the chosen tool invocation and any alternatives that were considered but skipped, and calls `emit_issue_body` when a session ends. Edge cases: empty rejected_actions list, evidence exceeding GitHub issue body limit (65536 chars), concurrent sessions.
test_plan: Unit-test `emit_issue_body` with a known DPOPair and assert the output string contains all four sections with correct markdown; test with empty rejected_actions and assert the Rejected section renders gracefully; test with evidence >65000 chars and assert truncation with a '... (truncated)' notice.
